content.full_text:
Farn Por-rrrcar PRACrrcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
December 15,2006
Shaun L. Farrell
City Treasurer
City of Galt
989 Elsworth Court
Galt, cA 95632
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. 4-06-215
Dear Mr. Farrell:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict of
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the .,Act,,).1
QUESTION
Would you have a conflict of interest in decisions to permit public funds in
certificates of deposit to "rol1 over" upon mafurity, or to withdraw those funds if market
conditions warrant a different investment strategy?
CONCLUSION
As we understand the facts, you would have a conflict of interest in these
decisions because it appears to be reasonably foreseeable that the decisions you describe
would have a material financial effect on your new employer.
FACTS
You have been employed by the city of Galt (.,Gait,,) in the capacity of city
Treasurer since August 6,1991. This is an elected, part-time position. The investment
strategy for Galt is determined by an investment committee made up of three members:
the City Treasurer, the City Manager, and the City's Finance Director. The investment
committee deposits City funds into local banks, provided that those institutions offer
competitive rates while maintaining the safety and liquidity of the investment.
' Govemment Code sections 8 1000-9 1 014. Commission regulations appear at trtle 2,sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
-cqai,


File No. A-06-215
Page No. 2
As of October l, 2006, Galt had investments in three local banks: $2 millio n in a
money market account at Union Bank of California, $8.5 million in CDs at Farmers and
Merchants Bank, and $8.5 million in CDs at Stockmans Bank. As City Treasu.er, yo,
will not be investing more of the City's portfolio in CDs because, according to the City,s
Investment Policy, the maximum allowable percentage of CDs per thatpoli""y has b"e,
reached' You will allow the existing CDs to "roll over" (i.e., continually renew) in those
institutions as long as the rate of interest remains competitive.
Galt's $8.5 million in Stockmans Bank is divided into three CDs. The first CD of
$3 million is scheduled to mature on January 26,2oo7,the second CD of $3 million on
Aprjl 26, 2007 , and the third cD of $2.5 million on May 29 , 2007 . The investment
committee plans no change to its investment strategy as to "local" CD accounts because
Galt has atotal of five banks and only two, Stockmans and Farmers, pay higher than
average interest rates on CDs.
On October 16, Stockmans hired you as apart-timeBusiness Development
Officer, with compensation limited to a salary. You receive no commissions on any
business you generate for the bank, and your compensation is not contingent on whether
Galt continues its business with the bank. The employee who determines interest rates on
CDs is the Chief Financial Officer ("CFo"). you have no responsibility in that area, you
do not report to the cFo, and your contact with him is timited.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict of interest rules generally prohibit a public official from taking
part in a governmental decision in which the official has u "onfli"t of interest, but the
possibility that an official may have conflicts of interest growing out of a new job does
not prevent the official from accepting that employment.T The loal of the Actls conflict
of interest provisions is simply to ensure that "fp]ublic officials, whether elected or
appointed, fshould] perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by
their own financial interests. . .." (Section SloOt(b).) Thus, section g7100 prohibits a
public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in *hi"h the official has i financial
inlerest, including financial interests in non-governmental employers which are sources
of income to the official.
'Pl"as" bear in mind that governmental.agencies may maintain lists of "incompatible activities,,,
which are not part of the Act. You should consrrlt your city Attorney to ,rru." that you do not engage in
outside employment that Galt has determined to bs incompatible witir your responsibilities to the city.


File No. A-06-215
Page No. 3
Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under the Act requires analysis
of the following questions:
1. Are you a "public official" within the meaning of the Act?
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to "public officials,,as that
term is defined by the Act. As City Treasurer, you are a public official subject to the
Act's conflict of interest provisions. (Section g2049.)
2' Would you be making, participating in making, or using your official position to
influence a goyernmental decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official , acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contrictual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulati on 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates,
advises or makes recommendations to a decisionmaker regarding a governmental
decision. (Regulation 15702.2.) A public official is atteripting to .rr" his or her official
position to influence a decision if for the purpose of influencing ttre decision, the official
contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her
agency. (Regulation 1 87 02.3.)
Your account of the facts indicates that you have the authority to vote to permit
Galt's CDs to "automatically" ro11 over in Stockmans, or to withdraw those funds and
place them elsewhere. In either case, whether you choose to maintain the status quo, or
to change course, you would be making governmental decisions.
3' What are your economic interests - the possible sources of a conflict of interest?
Under Section 87103 of the Act, there are six different types of economic interests
thatmay potentially give rise to a conflict of interest:
"(1) A public official has an economic interest in a business entity where the
public official either has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more
in the business entity. (Section g7103(a); regulation 1g703.1(a).)
"(2) A public official has an economic interest in a business entity where he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee or employee, or holis any position
in management. (Section g7103(d); regulation 13703.1.)


File No. A-06-215
Page No. 4
"(3) A public official has an economic interest in any real property in which
the public official has a direct or indirect interest *o.th $Z,OOO or more
in fair market value. (Section g7103(b); regulation 1g703.2.)
"(4) A public official has an economic interest in any person from whom helshe
has received income aggregating$500 within 12 months prior to the time
when the relevant governmental decision is made. (Section g7103(c);
regulation 18703.3.)
"(5) A public official has an economic interest in any donor of or any
intermediary or agent for a donor of a gift or giftr aggregatingd360 o, *or"
in value provided to, received by, or promised to the publi. oifi"iul within
12 months prior to t4e time when the decision is made. (Section g7103(e);
regulation 18703.4)3
"(6) A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal
finances' A governmental decision has a personal financial edct on a public
official if the decision will result in the peisonal expenses, income, assets, or
liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family increasing or
decreasing. (Section 87 lO3;regulation I g703.5.),,
You have asked us specifically about conflicts of interest arising from your
emploSzment with Stockmans. Assumingthatit is a business entity in JHch you have
neither an investment interest nor a managerial position, it will nonetheless become an
economic interest of y_ours as an employer, and as a source of income (once it has paid
you $500 or more within any 72-month period). You have not told us of any other
economic interests you may have, but since a public official always has an interest in his
or her personal finances, we will also considei this economic inteiest in our analysis.
4. Are your economic interests directly involved in the governmental decision?
A business entity that is a source of income is directly involved in a decision
before an official's agency when, directly or by an agent, it:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) rs a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding conceming the
decision before the official or the official's ug"r"y. A person is the subject of
a proceeding if a decision involves the issuan.", ."r"-ul, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person." (Regulation Ig7 04.1(a).),,
3 The current $360 gift limit will be increased to $390 effective January 1,2007,as a result of the
Commission's bienrual adjustment for inflation.


File No. A-06-215
Page No. 5
If a source of income is not directly involved in a govemmental decision, it is
treated as indirectly involved therein. (Regulation 18704.116).) A bank is directly
involved in decisions relating to a city's deposits inthatbank. you yourself would be
directly involved in these decisions if they were to have any financial effect on your
personal finances or those of your immediate family. (Regulation 1g704.5.)4
5. Materiality standard; what financial impact on your economic interest would be
considered material?
Not all financial effects are sufficient to create a conflict of interest as defined by
the Act; only "material" frnancial effects cause conflicts of interest. The size of any
financial effect that is considered to be "material" varies with the nature of the economic
interest, and whether that economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in the
decision at issue. For business entities that are sources of income, any ftnancial effect on
the entity is presumed to be mateial if the entity is directly involved ln ttre decision.
This presumptionmay be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decision will have any ftnancial effect on the entity. (Regulation 1g705. i.)
An impact on personal finances is considered to be "mateia|" iflt amounts to $250 or
more in any 72-month period. (Regulation 1g705.5.)
Finally, there is a separate materiality standard which applies in cases where there
is a "nexus" between duties owed by a public official to a source of income and to the
official's public agency. Your account of the facts indicates that there is not a.,nexus,,
between your public duties and your private employment, and this altemative standard is
in any case the same as the materiality standard for business entities directly involved in a
decision. (See regulation 1 8705.3(c).)
6. Foreseeability
Conflicts of interest do not arise if a material financial effect is not a reasonably
foreseeable consequence of a particular decision in which the public official participates.
An effect upon an economic interest is considered "reasonably foreseeable,,#there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FppC Ops. 19g.)
From what we know of the terms of your employment, it appears that it is not
reasonably foreseeable that decisions regarding the city;s investments in the bank would
have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on your personal finances. On
the other hand, it does appear that decisions regarding several million dollars of deposits
would inevitably have some financial effect on the depository bank. As noted aboie, any
financial effect on this business entity is presumed to be material. Thus we conclude that
a "Immediate family" is defined as the official's spouse and any dependant children.
(Section 82029.)


File No. A-06-215
Page No. 6
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions you describe would have amaterial
ftnancial effect on Stockmans.
7. and 8. Exceptions to the RuIe
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate in that decision if the circumstances are such that the "public generally,
exception may be invoked. This exception applies when the financial edect of a decision
upon a public official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect of the
decision on a significant segment of the public generally. (Section 87l13;regulation
18707(a).) Since the effects of the decisions you describe would be unique tJstockmans,
the "public generally" exception would seem to have no application in this case.
Finally, an official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate in that decision if the "legally required participation" exception is applicable.
(Section 87101, regulation 18708.) This is an exception ihut typically applies -h"r, u,
agency is unable to assemble a quorum of its members withouiit 
" purti"ipation of an
official who has a conflict of interest. You have not indicated that other decisionmakers
would be unavailable to decide the city's course in this instance, but we mention this
exception for the sake of completeness.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel ,tuu
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
LTWjgl
